title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text: The systematic classification of living organisms has fascinated scholars for centuries, yet it was not until the eighteenth century that a truly (0) comprehensive framework emerged. The Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus is widely (1) with establishing the binomial nomenclature system, a method that assigns each species a two-part Latin name. This elegant approach not only brought (2) to the chaotic naming conventions of the time, but also allowed scientists across different countries to communicate without ambiguity. Before Linnaeus, naturalists had (3) to describe organisms using long, cumbersome phrases that varied from one region to another, making collaboration extremely difficult.||The Linnaean system works by (4) organisms into a hierarchy of groups, ranging from the broad category of kingdom down to individual species. Each level, or taxon, (5) a progressively narrower set of shared characteristics. Despite its enduring influence, modern taxonomy has not been (6) unchanged. The rise of molecular biology has led scientists to reconsider traditional classifications, as genetic analysis sometimes (7) unexpected relationships between species that look quite different on the surface.||Today, taxonomists must (8) both morphological features and genetic data when describing new species. This dual approach has resulted in a far more accurate picture of the tree of life, though it has also introduced new complexity into a discipline that Linnaeus himself might barely recognise.

example.explanation: "Comprehensive framework" is the standard academic collocation for a complete and systematic structure. "Thorough" and "detailed" describe methods rather than frameworks, and "widespread" refers to extent, not depth.

questions.explanation: "Credited with" is the fixed phrase used when attributing an achievement or discovery to a person. The others do not collocate with "with" in this sense of attribution.
"Bring order to chaos" is a fixed idiomatic expression. While "structure" and "logic" are related concepts, they do not form the conventional collocation with "bring" in this context.
"Resorted to" is the fixed phrasal verb meaning to use something as a last option. "Turned to" is possible but less precise; the others do not collocate with "to" in this context.

"Grouping organisms into a hierarchy" is the standard scientific collocation. "Sorting" is informal, "placing" lacks the collective sense, and "arranging" does not convey classification into defined categories.

"Represents a set of characteristics" is the correct academic collocation, conveying that the taxon stands for or embodies those traits. The others suggest physical containment rather than conceptual representation.

"Left unchanged" is the standard passive construction meaning something has not been altered. "Kept unchanged" is possible but requires a subject doing the keeping; the passive construction here requires "left".
"Reveals unexpected relationships" is the precise academic collocation. "Discovers" implies a deliberate search, "exposes" has a negative connotation, and "shows" is too informal for this context.

"Consider both X and Y" is the natural academic construction for taking multiple factors into account. "Account for" requires a preposition and different syntax, "weigh" implies comparison rather than consideration, and "assess" focuses on evaluation rather than inclusion.
